Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle (31): So What Do You Want to Learn Next?
Python
JavaScript
13th iThome Ironman Contest

I finally crossed the finish line of the Ironman contest. So for day 31, the last one, I'm just going to sit down and chat with you about how the whole thing went — what I learned about Python coming from JavaScript, and why I think the real takeaway is to keep learning no matter what tool you pick up.
/en/python/2021/09/16/learn-python-from-javascript-15-modules/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (15): Modules
第 13 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

Let's talk about modules in Python. JavaScript has its own idea of modules too, so we'll compare the two side by side — how to split your code into a separate file, export it, and import it back. Plus a couple of handy built-ins, dir() and help(), that make exploring modules way easier.
/en/python/2021/09/13/learn-python-from-javascript-12-operators/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (12) - Operators

Operators are something you use constantly, so there's no skipping them in the basics. We'll look at the arithmetic operators, the surprising Python 2 vs Python 3 division difference, the floor division operator, merging lists with +, and the operator precedence gotcha that shows up in JavaScript interviews.
/en/python/2021/09/08/learn-python-from-javascript-07-conditionals-and-loops/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (7) — Conditionals and Loops

Next up: conditionals and loops, since you basically can't write any program without these two. We'll line up JavaScript's if/else against Python's if/elif/else, talk about the new match statement in Python 3.10, and cover for and while loops including how to fake a do...while in Python.
/en/python/2021/09/02/learn-python-from-javascript-01-intro/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Dev's Angle (1) - Table of Contents and Rambling

This is my third Ironman challenge this year and my fourth Ironman series overall. Honestly I'm a little scared I won't finish this time, because this series is pretty much learning Python from scratch — but from a JavaScript developer's point of view. Here's the table of contents and a bit of rambling to kick things off.
